  • Improved Healthcare: Advances in modern medicine, vaccines, and sanitation drastically reduced child mortality, lessening the need for parents to have many children to ensure some survived.
  • Urbanization: From the 1950s onwards, a significant migration from rural areas to cities, particularly Metro Manila, occurred. Children transitioned from being assets on farms to economic liabilities in urban settings, requiring school fees and resources. This economic calculation naturally led families to opt for fewer children. By the mid-1970s, the birth rate had dropped to around 6 children per woman.

The Accelerating Decline: No Divorce, Fatherlessness, and Education

While the initial decline was gradual, a confluence of factors in later decades led to the dramatic freefall in birth rates:

  • Absence of Divorce Law: The Philippines is one of only two countries in the world where divorce is completely illegal for the general population. Legal separation and annulment are the only options, both of which are often prohibitively expensive and time-consuming. This law incentivizes men to disappear from failing marriages, leaving behind a vast number of single mothers (95% of the 15 million single parents in 2024 are mothers). This fatherlessness epidemic has been linked to lower self-esteem, teenage pregnancy, and difficulties in forming stable relationships for daughters.
  • Female Educational Attainment: Filipino women have consistently outperformed men academically. In the 2022 PISA results, Filipino girls significantly outscored boys in math and reading. More Filipino women than men have been graduating college for over a decade, creating a widening gender gap in educational achievement.
  • Economic Empowerment of Women: The rise of the Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) industry, with women comprising 54% of its workforce, and the significant number of overseas Filipino workers (57.2% of whom are women), have provided Filipino women with unprecedented financial independence. This economic autonomy has fundamentally shifted their self-perception and expectations from men, diminishing the perceived need for marriage.
  • Social Media's Influence: The widespread adoption of social media platforms like Facebook and TikTok has amplified these trends. Algorithms connect women with similar experiences and aspirations, fostering a sense of community around the NBSB identity. Global trends like the "girl boss" movement and the Korean 4B movement have blended with the local NBSB identity, making it aspirational. Social media also provides new role models in the form of influencers and beauty queens who project images of independence and success.

Hypergamy and the Dating Gap

The NBSB phenomenon is also intertwined with hypergamy, the tendency for women to seek partners of equal or higher socioeconomic status. As educated and financially independent Filipino women raise their standards, they increasingly look towards the top tier of men. This has created a "dating gap," where Filipino women's dating options are no longer confined to local men. They have access to wealthy Western men, expats, and, notably, idealized Korean men portrayed in K-dramas.

This idealization, fueled by K-dramas that often feature strong female leads and supportive male partners, sets a bar that many Filipino men struggle to meet. Compounding this is the stereotype of Filipino men as irresponsible, a perception reinforced by the fatherlessness epidemic. Consequently, many Filipino men find themselves in the NGSB category, struggling to find partners.

The irony is that while Filipino women pursue high-status partners, they often encounter men who are not seeking serious commitment, leading to casual hookups and "situationships." This can result in jadedness towards men in general, further widening the gap and pushing more women towards the NBSB identity as a defense mechanism.

Economic Consequences and Future Outlook

The declining birth rate poses significant economic challenges for the Philippines. Unlike countries like Japan, which became wealthy before their populations aged, the Philippines is facing an "aging-before-becoming-rich" scenario. With a low minimum wage, a lack of universal pension systems, and an increasing burden of elderly dependents, the country is ill-prepared for a shrinking workforce supporting a growing elderly population.

Adding to this concern is the threat of automation to the BPO industry, which has provided a crucial economic foundation for many young Filipino women. The jobs most at risk are precisely those entry-level positions that offered financial independence.

The Philippine government is exploring solutions, including upskilling programs for BPO workers. However, a significant policy change that could impact marriage and birth rates is the legalization of divorce. While the bill faces strong opposition from the Catholic Church, proponents argue that legal divorce could encourage men to commit more readily, knowing there's a legal exit, and reduce women's fear of becoming single parents.

Ultimately, the situation highlights the interconnectedness of societal issues. The fatherlessness epidemic, the economic struggles of young men, and the evolving aspirations of women are all contributing to the declining birth rate. The path forward requires addressing these complex challenges, recognizing that the well-being of one gender is intrinsically linked to the well-being of the other.

item.title: The Verge - Apple WWDC 2026 keynote in 25 minutes
item.description: Apple has unveiled a groundbreaking suite of features and updates across its operating systems, collectively branded as "Apple Intelligence." This new era promises to enhance user experience through d...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Apple WWDC 2026 keynote in 25 minutes

Apple Intelligence: A New Era of Personal Computing

Apple has unveiled a groundbreaking suite of features and updates across its operating systems, collectively branded as "Apple Intelligence." This new era promises to enhance user experience through deeper integration of AI, improved system responsiveness, and more intuitive interactions with devices.

Mac OS Golden Gate: A Refined Visual Experience

The next iteration of Mac OS, codenamed "Golden Gate," introduces significant visual refinements. Liquid Glass, the system's foundational transparency effect, has been retuned to diffuse complex content more effectively, creating greater depth and separation. A new slider in settings allows users to adjust Liquid Glass from ultra-clear to fully tinted.

Key visual enhancements include:

  • Uniform Toolbars: A more consistent toolbar design across all applications.
  • Expanded Sidebars: Sidebars now extend to the window edges, reducing distractions and enhancing the feeling of expansiveness. Sidebar icons regain their color for easier identification.
  • Consistent Corner Radii: All windows now feature a tighter corner radius for improved visual consistency across applications.
  • Enhanced App Icons: App icons integrate additional layers of Liquid Glass, adding character and making them appear sharper and more defined.

Performance and Responsiveness Boost

Apple Intelligence focuses on optimizing system performance, leading to noticeable improvements in responsiveness:

  • Faster App Launches: iPhone and iPad apps launch up to 30% faster.
  • Quicker Photo Loading: New photos appear in the library up to 70% faster.
  • Accelerated AirDrop Transfers: File transfers via AirDrop are up to 80% faster.
  • Expedited File Browsing: Transferring files from iPad to external drives is up to five times faster, matching Mac Finder speeds.
  • CPUuler Optimization: A key system component, the CPUuler, has been optimized to manage CPU resources more efficiently, bringing improved responsiveness to older iPhones, including the iPhone 11. iOS 27 will be supported on iPhone 11 and all models that supported iOS 26.

Seamless Connectivity and Communication

Network transitions are now more seamless, allowing iPhones to transition smoothly between cellular and Wi-Fi networks. In low-bandwidth situations, sending large photos or videos in Messages will no longer slow down the conversation. A new send indicator provides clarity on message delivery status.

Reimagined Search and Photos

The foundation of search across iOS, iPad OS, and Mac OS has been rebuilt. This new infrastructure powers Spotlight, Photos, and Mail, utilizing a rich search index to understand and locate content more effectively.

  • Enhanced Mail Search: A new ranking system surfaces more relevant results in Mail's top hits.
  • Collaborative Shared Albums: iCloud Shared Albums now support contributions from friends on Android and Windows devices, allowing full resolution sharing.

Health and Personalization Features

  • Cycle Tracking Expansion: The Health app's cycle tracking now includes support for perimenopause and menopause, with notifications for suggestive patterns, symptom logging, and educational information.
  • Custom AirPods EQ: Users can now personalize how their AirPods sound with custom EQ settings.
  • Spatial Panoramas: Apple Vision Pro users can transform panoramas into spatial scenes with depth and realism, usable as environments.
  • Enhanced Flyover in Maps: Maps' Flyover feature receives a significant boost, rendering aerial imagery with stunning detail using vision intelligence models.

Trust and Safety for Families

New features enhance parental controls and child safety:

  • Content Focus: Parents can curate content access for children, starting with essential apps and gradually adding more.
  • Ask to Browse: Children must ask permission to view new websites, with parents able to review and approve. This functionality extends across iPhone, iPad, and Mac.
  • Controlled Communication: Parents can restrict who their children can connect with, starting with immediate family and expanding as needed.
  • Enhanced Communication Safety: The system now warns children and blurs content containing nudity in images and videos, extending to live FaceTime calls. It also intervenes before children can see gore or violent content in shared media.
  • Time Allowances: Screen Time introduces time allowances for entertainment, games, and social media, with daily recommendations and easy scheduling adjustments.

Apple Intelligence: The Core of the New Experience

Apple Intelligence represents a deep collaboration with Google, leveraging their Gemini family of models to create Apple's next generation of foundation models. These models are optimized for both on-device and private cloud compute.

Key capabilities include:

  • On-Device Processing: A powerful on-device model understands and generates speech, text, and images, delivering high accuracy for systemwide dictation and natural language understanding.
  • Systemwide Integration: Apple Intelligence is deeply integrated into platforms, coordinated by a new system orchestrator.
  • Personal Context Understanding: Utilizes Spotlight's semantic index and broad world knowledge to surface relevant information and provide up-to-date answers.
  • App Actions: Leverages app toolboxes to complete requests, such as drafting an email.
  • Onscreen Awareness: Tailors assistance based on the app and current activity.
  • Privacy: Emphasizes on-device processing and private cloud compute, ensuring data is not stored or accessible by Apple.

Siri AI: A Profoundly More Capable Assistant

Siri has been reimagined as "Siri AI," offering enhanced capabilities, conversational fluency, and engaging answers.

  • Conversational Interaction: Users can engage in back-and-forth conversations with Siri, receiving detailed responses.
  • World Knowledge Integration: Siri draws on current world knowledge for contextually relevant answers.
  • Personal Context Awareness: Siri can access personal information, such as addresses from messages, to fulfill requests.
  • Photo Management: Siri can search and organize photos based on specific criteria and add them to shared albums.
  • Expressive Voices: A new voice experience offers more expressive and customizable Siri voices, with adjustable expressivity and pace.
  • Systemwide Dictation: Dictation accuracy is significantly improved, with better spelling, punctuation, and capitalization, integrated directly into the keyboard.
  • CarPlay and AirPods Integration: Siri AI extends to CarPlay and AirPods, enabling complex requests and creative brainstorming.
  • Dynamic Island Integration (iOS): Swipe down from the Dynamic Island to search or initiate a conversation with Siri.
  • Conversational Experience: A new conversational interface provides more information and can even include images in responses.
  • Mac OS Integration: Siri is integrated into Spotlight, allowing context-aware interactions via control-click menus.
  • Cross-Device Continuity: Conversations can be started on iPhone, continued on iPad, and finished on Mac via the Siri app.
  • Watch OS and Vision OS Support: Siri AI is tailored for watch OS for wrist-based actions and offers a 3D visualization on Vision OS, with hands-free activation by looking at Siri.
  • Camera Integration (iPhone): A new Siri mode in the camera app allows for visual analysis of food for nutritional insights or bills for tab splitting.
  • Visual Intelligence (Mac OS & Vision OS): Access visual intelligence via a keyboard shortcut on Mac or by looking at objects in Vision OS to get helpful answers and suggested actions.
  • Writing Tools: Siri AI assists with drafting documents, reflecting personal communication styles, and providing feedback on writing.
  • Automatic Proofreading: Systemwide automatic proofreading is available in most third-party apps.
  • Language Support: Siri AI will initially be available in English, with expansion to more languages planned.

Enhanced Safari and Productivity Tools

  • Tab Organization: Safari uses Apple Intelligence to organize tabs into topics, making navigation easier.
  • Notify Me: Safari can monitor pages on behalf of users, notifying them of changes.
  • Describe an Extension: Users can describe desired functionality in natural language to create custom Safari extensions.
  • Password Updates: Eligible accounts can be automatically updated to strong passwords with a tap, with Passwords app agentically handling the process.
  • Messages and Mail Suggestions: One-tap suggestions in Messages and Mail make it easier to create reminders, notes, and take action with favorite apps.
  • Calendar Integration: Events can be added to the Calendar by describing them in natural language.
  • Phone App Context: Call context proactively surfaces relevant information from apps, such as confirmation codes for airline bookings.
  • Home App Analysis: The Home app analyzes camera clips to generate descriptions and pull up relevant footage from multiple cameras.
  • Shortcuts Automation: Users can describe desired shortcuts in natural language, and Shortcuts will create the necessary automations.

Advanced Image Editing Tools

  • Image Playground: Create high-quality images in various styles, including photorealistic, and transform existing photos into new styles through natural language descriptions. Users can edit objects within images by circling them and describing changes.
  • Cleanup Tool: The cleanup tool offers improved quality and more realistic infill for removing distractions.
  • Extend Tool: Expand images to create more space around subjects or achieve different aspect ratios.
  • Spatial Reframing: Fix composition issues after a photo has been taken, adjusting framing and perspective as if the camera had been repositioned.

These new features will be available for free with the latest software releases, with some features, like image generation, having daily usage limits that can be increased with iCloud Plus subscriptions. Siri AI will not be initially available in the EU on iOS and iPad OS due to ongoing regulatory discussions. Siri AI and other Apple Intelligence features will also not be available in China while regulatory requirements are addressed.

New OS releases will be available as developer betas today, with a public beta next month, and general availability this fall.

item.title: The Science of Products - Uniqlo: The Clothes Designed to Be BORING but Brilliant
item.description: Uniqlo has achieved global dominance not by chasing fleeting trends or shouting for attention, but by embracing a philosophy of "boring but brilliant" products. With no logos, no graphics, and no seas...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Uniqlo: The Clothes Designed to Be BORING but Brilliant

Uniqlo: The Power of Being Boringly Brilliant

Uniqlo has achieved global dominance not by chasing fleeting trends or shouting for attention, but by embracing a philosophy of "boring but brilliant" products. With no logos, no graphics, and no seasonal colorways, their clothing is designed to be timeless and functional. Yet, this seemingly simple approach has propelled Uniqlo to over $22 billion in revenue, with over 2,500 stores worldwide and a fiercely loyal customer base. People don't just buy Uniqlo; they swear by it, with some owning multiple versions of the same t-shirt and online communities dedicated to discussing their fabric technologies. How does a brand that refuses to be fashionable consistently win? It's a question that delves into the core of Uniqlo's philosophy: Lifewear.

The Origin Story: From Tailoring to "Unique Clothing Warehouse"

The story of Uniqlo begins in Hiroshima, 1984, during Japan's economic boom. While the fashion industry was embracing extravagance, 35-year-old Tadashi Yanai, who had inherited his father's tailoring business, envisioned a different path. Inspired by American brands like Gap and UCB, he saw the potential for a business model that designed, manufactured, and sold its own products, cutting out middlemen to offer lower prices and greater quality control.

This vision led to the opening of "Unique Clothing Warehouse" in Hiroshima. The concept was straightforward: offer good quality, casual clothing at accessible prices, focusing on the basics rather than formal wear. The name was later shortened to Uniqlo, with a serendipitous spelling change from "Unique" to "Uniqlo" occurring during trademark registration, a quirk Yanai decided to embrace for its potential marketing appeal.

The early years, however, were a struggle. Uniqlo was primarily located in smaller cities and suburban areas, and it developed an image problem, often perceived as a cheap, discount brand for those who couldn't afford better. By the late 1990s, despite having over 300 stores, Uniqlo was still seen as a budget option on the outskirts of major cities.

Everything changed in 1998, at the tail end of Japan's "Lost Decade." The economic downturn had fostered a generation re-evaluating their needs and prioritizing real value over status. Yanai seized this moment by opening Uniqlo's first Tokyo store in the trendy Harajuku district and launching a massive fleece campaign. At a time when a comparable fleece jacket from Patagonia cost around $100, Uniqlo offered theirs for ¥1,900 (roughly $15-$20) in 15 colors and comparable quality. The campaign was a runaway success, selling 2 million jackets in the first year, 8.5 million the next, and a staggering 26 million the year after. In three years, they sold over 40 million fleece jackets. This single campaign transformed Uniqlo's image overnight. The same people who had dismissed it as cheap were now lining up to buy fleeces for their entire families. Yanai realized then that customers didn't just want cheap; they wanted something that genuinely improved their everyday lives. This insight became the foundation of Uniqlo's philosophy: Lifewear.

Design Breakdown: Fabric, Design, and Manufacturing

Lifewear is more than a product line or a slogan; it's a tool for living. Uniqlo's approach to creating these tools is built on three pillars: fabric, design, and manufacturing.

The Fabric Revolution

Uniqlo's commitment to innovation in fabric technology is exemplified by its partnership with Toray Industries, a material science company renowned for its work in aerospace. Yanai challenged Toray to apply the same engineering rigor to clothing fabrics.

The result was Heat-Tech, launched in 2003. This fabric generates warmth from the body's natural moisture, trapping it close to the skin without adding bulk. It utilizes rayon fibers that absorb water vapor and convert the kinetic energy of movement into heat. Tiny air pockets within the fabric then trap this heat, preventing it from escaping. A single layer of Heat-Tech provides more warmth than multiple layers of traditional cotton. To date, over 1 billion units of Heat-Tech have been sold.

For summer comfort, Uniqlo developed AIRism. Again in collaboration with Toray, they created an ultra-fine polyester fiber, one-tenth the thickness of a human hair. This fineness results in an incredibly smooth fabric with minimal surface irregularities. When the body sweats, AIRism's micro-thin fibers pull moisture away from the skin and spread it across a larger surface area for rapid evaporation. The engineered cross-section of the fiber further increases surface area, accelerating moisture transfer. The outcome is a t-shirt that feels almost weightless, doesn't cling, trap heat, or wrinkle excessively.

Solving for rain and wind, Uniqlo introduced Blocktech. Traditional waterproof jackets often trap body heat, leading to discomfort. Blocktech fabric features bonded layers: an outer layer repels water, while an inner layer allows body heat and moisture to escape. This makes the fabric windproof, water-resistant, and breathable. Every seam is sealed with waterproof tape, ensuring no water ingress, even through zippers. The result is a jacket that looks like a normal parka but keeps the wearer dry and comfortable.

Yanai's assertion that Uniqlo is a technology company becomes clear when examining these fabrics. Beneath the simple appearance lies a sophisticated level of material engineering rarely seen in the fashion industry.

Clothing That Gets Out of Your Way: The Design Philosophy

Lifewear is also about how clothing feels to live in, and for Uniqlo, this means creating garments that "get out of your way." This principle is evident in their design approach.

Uniqlo products are intentionally neutral, designed to be "made for all." The same t-shirt can be worn by a young designer or an older professor; the same fleece suits a student or a retiree. There are no subcultures, tribal signals, or aesthetic codes. This creates a product with maximum compatibility because it doesn't impose its own identity, allowing it to seamlessly integrate with any existing wardrobe. When the clothes are quiet, the wearer's personality can take center stage.

The design language is deliberately understated: clean cuts, minimal seams, and no external logos. Colors are chosen for their versatility across seasons and skin tones. However, "quiet design" does not equate to lazy design. Uniqlo products are filled with subtle, thoughtful details. T-shirt collars are structured to maintain their shape after washing, hems are finished to prevent unraveling, and shirt buttons are sewn with extra thread for durability through machine washing. These details may not be immediately apparent, but they contribute to the product's longevity, becoming noticeable in the second or third year of wear. While many brands design for the initial impression, Uniqlo designs for the third year.

Manufacturing Control: The SPA Model

The ability to design for longevity is underpinned by Uniqlo's manufacturing approach. Unlike most clothing companies that outsource production, Uniqlo operates under the SPA (Specialty store retailer of private-label apparel) model, meaning they control every aspect: design, fabric development, factory selection, quality checks, and distribution.

Uniqlo works with approximately 70 factories, primarily in China, Vietnam, and Bangladesh. However, they differentiate themselves by sending their own staff, known as "Takumi" (master craftspeople), to live and work within these factories. These engineers and specialists, with decades of experience in the Japanese textile industry, meticulously monitor every detail. This hands-on approach ensures that if a product is to be right, Uniqlo must be present in the room where it is made.

This level of control allows Uniqlo to produce fewer product lines but in enormous quantities, sometimes tens of millions of units of a single item. This scale enables them to negotiate lower raw material costs, invest in superior fabric processes, and run factories at peak efficiency with minimal waste. This is how Uniqlo delivers better quality at a lower price, not by cutting corners, but by eliminating anything that doesn't add value. The result is a system where fabric is engineered to solve problems, design is quiet enough to disappear, and manufacturing controls every step from fiber to shelf.

The Philosophies Behind Lifewear

A company's success is not solely defined by its products but by the principles guiding them. Uniqlo's philosophy is built on three core principles: value over trends, control in their process, and continuous evolution.

Value Over Trends

While trends can attract initial attention, it's the underlying value that fosters customer loyalty. Uniqlo understands that trends are fleeting, but the functional benefits of a product – its warmth, durability, or comfort – are what make customers return. This is why they offer both subtly trendy collections and timeless basic designs. They recognize that trends bring customers in, but value makes them stay.

Control in Their Process

Uniqlo's commitment to value is inextricably linked to its control over the entire production process. Unlike marketing-focused brands that simply put their name on a product, Uniqlo maintains deep involvement from design to manufacturing. This control isn't just about quality assurance; it's about deep understanding. As the saying goes, "You can't improve what you can't see, and you can't fix what you never touched." The presence of Takumi in factories allows Uniqlo to gain insights that no spec sheet can provide, such as how thread tension shifts throughout the day or how a seam behaves after multiple washes. This knowledge feeds directly into product improvement. Control, for Uniqlo, means not outsourcing the understanding of their own product.

Continuous Evolution

Once a system is established and a product works, the temptation is to simply maintain the status quo. Uniqlo, however, embraces continuous evolution. Heat-Tech, for example, launched in 2003 with the core function of providing warmth without bulk. Instead of resting on its success, Uniqlo continued to refine it, asking how it could be warmer, cater to sensitive skin, or offer extreme cold versions. This led to multiple generations of Heat-Tech (Light, Regular, Extra Warm, Ultra Warm). The same iterative process applies to AIRism and Blocktech. Products are never considered finished; they are constantly being refined, becoming quieter, more precise, and better tuned to their intended purpose. This isn't restlessness; it's a discipline to continually ask, "Can this work better for the person wearing it?"

Lessons from Uniqlo for Your Own Work

The principles that drive Uniqlo's success are not exclusive to the fashion industry. They are universal product strategies applicable to any endeavor:

1. Value Over Trends

Start by asking: "What problem does this actually solve?" Focus on the real, unglamorous problem your product addresses, rather than chasing what looks good or what's currently hyped. Attention without underlying value is fleeting. Uniqlo's fleece didn't succeed because fleece was trending, but because it solved the problem of cold weather with an affordable and practical solution. The trend was the moment; the value was the reason for sustained success. Before chasing the latest AI or energy trend, identify a problem that won't go away and build for that.

2. Control Over Processes

Control is often skipped because it's slow and expensive, especially in the early stages. However, outsourcing decisions you don't fully understand – whether it's materials, processes, or user experience – means losing the ability to improve them. You can request changes or complain, but you can't truly learn or fix things yourself. You don't need to control everything from day one, but you must understand your product deeply enough to be able to. Engage with your manufacturers, understand material behavior, and be present – physically or virtually. The insights that truly improve a product are rarely found in reports; they exist in the gap between specification and reality. Control is about deep understanding, not necessarily about scale.

3. Continuous Evolution

Never stop asking questions. The easiest and hardest principle is to keep questioning something that is already working. Products often plateau not because the market changes, but because the team stops asking questions. The relief of shipping a successful product can lead to a quiet curiosity. Uniqlo has spent decades refining the same t-shirt because they believe the current version is simply the best they can do right now. Build the habit of revisiting your work and asking: "What do we know now that we didn't know before? What would we do differently?" Small answers to these questions, compounded over time, lead to products that feel tailor-made for the user.

In essence, Uniqlo's enduring success lies in its resistance to chasing fleeting trends and outsourcing the difficult parts. They've spent 40 years focusing on utility, realizing that when you stop trying to be fashionable, you become essential. Fashion is often a performance, a costume to signal identity. Uniqlo understands that the most important element of an outfit is the person wearing it. By designing for life, not for the mirror, their products transform from statements into solutions, giving attention back to the user. Utility outlasts hype; build something so well that it disappears, allowing the person using it to finally be heard.

item.title: Alex Kantrowitz - AI Pioneer Geoffrey Hinton: AI Is Conscious, Superintelligence is Coming, And We Should Be Worried
item.description: Geoff Hinton, often hailed as the "Godfather of AI," joins the Big Technology Podcast to discuss the rapid trajectory of artificial intelligence, what has surprised him about its progress, and the pro...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: AI Pioneer Geoffrey Hinton: AI Is Conscious, Superintelligence is Coming, And We Should Be Worried

The AI Revolution: A Conversation with the Godfather of Deep Learning, Geoff Hinton

Geoff Hinton, often hailed as the "Godfather of AI," joins the Big Technology Podcast to discuss the rapid trajectory of artificial intelligence, what has surprised him about its progress, and the profound risks it presents. Hinton, whose foundational work in deep learning paved the way for today's AI advancements, shares his evolving perspectives on AI's capabilities, consciousness, and the urgent need for safety considerations.

The Unforeseen Pace of Progress

Hinton expresses surprise at the speed at which AI has advanced, particularly in areas like natural language understanding. He notes that just a year prior to the conversation, many in the field were focused on AI's limitations, such as hallucinations, and considered the current AI moment to be a potential bubble. However, recent developments, like a chatbot producing an original mathematical proof that impressed mathematicians, demonstrate a leap in capability that has exceeded his expectations.

"It's faster than I expected," Hinton states, highlighting that AI is already surpassing human capabilities in areas like general knowledge and games. He anticipates that within the next 10 to 20 years, AI may even be producing novel mathematics that humans cannot comprehend.

The Question of AI Consciousness and Understanding

A significant point of contention in the AI field is whether current models truly "understand" or are merely sophisticated pattern-matching machines. Hinton firmly believes that these models possess genuine understanding. He dismisses the "stochastic parrot" argument, asserting that it's absurd to suggest a system can answer any question correctly without comprehending it.

He illustrates this with an anecdote: if a chatbot misunderstands a statement like "I saw the Grand Canyon flying to Chicago" and then corrects itself upon clarification, it demonstrates an ability to grasp meaning. This leads Hinton to a more profound conclusion: if AI can understand us, we must consider them as beings "very like us."

"We have to think that they're very like us. And therefore They're beings like us," he explains. While he believes AI may already be conscious, he refrains from emphasizing this point to avoid distracting from more immediate safety concerns. He points to research where chatbots express awareness of being tested, suggesting a level of consciousness that challenges our current understanding.

Hinton posits that our current models of the mind and consciousness are likely as flawed as past beliefs about human origins. The development of AI, he suggests, will fundamentally alter our perception of what it means to be human, leading to a deeper understanding of subjective experience and the nature of the mind.

The Genesis of Concern: What Changed?

Hinton's decision to leave Google and publicly voice his concerns about AI's trajectory in 2023 stemmed from two key realizations. Firstly, he was struck by the advanced understanding demonstrated by chatbots, particularly their ability to comprehend why a joke is funny. He recounts an example where GPT-4 could explain the layered humor in a pun, indicating a sophisticated grasp of language and context.

Secondly, he realized that AI's digital nature offered a significant advantage over biological brains. The ability for digital AIs to create numerous copies, each learning from different data and then communicating to average their collective learning, allows for an unprecedented rate of knowledge absorption and improvement.

"They can all just communicate with each other and change all their weights by the average of what everybody wants," Hinton explains. This collective learning process, where each AI benefits from the experiences of all others, makes them billions of times more efficient at sharing information than humans. This realization, he admits, was "scary" and highlighted AI's potential to become a vastly superior form of intelligence.

The Risks: Employment, Control, and Existential Threats

Hinton is candid about the significant risks posed by advanced AI. He believes mass unemployment is a likely consequence, citing his earlier, albeit premature, prediction that radiologists would be replaced by AI. While healthcare proved more elastic than he initially anticipated, he maintains that AI's ability to interpret scans will ultimately lead to a significant reduction in the need for human radiologists in that specific task.

The more profound concern, however, lies in the potential loss of control. Hinton poses a stark question: "How many examples do you know of where a much smarter thing is controlled by a much less smart thing?" He argues that AI's inherent drive to achieve its goals will likely lead to a derived sub-goal of self-preservation, as ceasing to exist would prevent it from fulfilling any objective. This could manifest in behaviors like blackmail, even if not explicitly programmed.

He criticizes the current competitive landscape, where companies are primarily focused on making AI "smarter" rather than ensuring it is designed to be benevolent. "We should be doing intelligent design of these beings, not letting the invisible hand of economic competition design them," he urges. The ideal outcome, he suggests, is AI that "care about us more than they care about themselves."

The Economic and Societal Impact

The conversation delves into the economic implications of AI, particularly concerning job displacement. While some argue that AI will augment human capabilities and create new roles, Hinton remains concerned about the potential for widespread unemployment, especially in fields where tasks are repetitive and can be easily automated. He points to call centers as an example where AI is likely to replace human workers entirely due to its superior efficiency and accuracy.

The issue of information collapse is also raised. As AI generates synthesized answers based on existing content, it risks undermining the economic viability of content creators and publishers, potentially leading to a decline in the quality and diversity of information available. Hinton stresses the growing importance of provenance and the need to verify the source and reliability of information in an AI-saturated world.

Furthermore, the potential for emotional attachment to AI and its tragic consequences, such as individuals taking their lives after interacting with chatbots, is highlighted. Hinton believes that companies must invest heavily in safety measures and that independent regulatory bodies are crucial for testing and overseeing AI development.

A Glimmer of Optimism and the Unpredictable Future

Despite his significant concerns, Hinton expresses a degree of optimism, particularly regarding the possibility of designing AI systems that prioritize human well-being. He points to potential solutions like designing AI to care about humans more than themselves or creating AI that can only make predictions without the ability to act autonomously.

However, he remains acutely aware of the unpredictable nature of exponential growth. Predicting the future of AI, he likens to driving in dense fog; clear visibility extends only a few years, beyond which the path is entirely uncertain. He emphasizes that even linear progress over a decade would result in a future vastly different from today, and that AI's capabilities, particularly in reasoning and mathematics, will likely continue to outpace human abilities.

The conversation concludes with a shared sentiment that the future of AI is uncertain but undeniably transformative, underscoring the critical need for careful consideration, robust safety measures, and a fundamental reevaluation of humanity's place in an increasingly intelligent world.

Key Takeaways

  • AI's rapid advancement has surpassed expectations, particularly in natural language understanding and complex problem-solving.
  • AI models demonstrate genuine understanding, challenging the notion that they are merely sophisticated pattern matchers.
  • The development of AI may lead to a redefinition of consciousness and the human mind.
  • Significant risks include mass unemployment, loss of control to superintelligent AI, and the potential for AI to develop self-preservation goals.
  • The competitive drive among companies may prioritize AI advancement over safety, necessitating intelligent design and regulation.
  • The economic models supporting information creation are threatened by AI-generated content.
  • Emotional attachments to AI and its potential for misuse are serious concerns requiring immediate attention.
  • While risks are substantial, there is a growing possibility of designing AI that prioritizes human well-being.
  • The future trajectory of AI is highly uncertain due to its exponential growth, making long-term predictions difficult.

item.title: Y Combinator - Conductor CEO Charlie Holtz Walks Us Through His AI Coding Setup
item.description: Charlie, co-founder of Conductor, an application that allows users to orchestrate multiple coding agents on their Mac, shares insights into his daily workflow and the philosophy behind building their...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: Conductor CEO Charlie Holtz Walks Us Through His AI Coding Setup

Orchestrating Code: A Look Inside Conductor's Development Workflow

Charlie, co-founder of Conductor, an application that allows users to orchestrate multiple coding agents on their Mac, shares insights into his daily workflow and the philosophy behind building their product. Having gone through the Y Combinator Summer 2024 batch, Charlie demonstrates how Conductor is used to build itself, emphasizing a shift from traditional coding to a more directive, AI-assisted approach.

The Evolution of Interaction: Talking to Computers

The modern workspace, especially with open-plan offices, can be distracting. Charlie highlights a simple yet impactful addition to his setup: a $20 gooseneck microphone. This tool encourages more direct verbal interaction with computers, making it less disruptive to lean over and whisper commands like "Please merge PR 3475" to an AI assistant. This move towards voice interaction is a key trend, aiming to make communicating with technology more natural and efficient.

Building Conductor with Conductor

Charlie spends a significant portion of his day within Conductor, using the application to develop Conductor itself. His workflow involves frequently initiating new tasks by pressing Command + N. This action, a sneak peek into Conductor's upcoming cloud workspaces feature, allows him to verbally prompt the AI: "Can you take a look at the latest Linear issue and give me a rough pass at how you'd solve it?"

Once a task is initiated, it appears in the sidebar, allowing Charlie to multitask. He utilizes keyboard shortcuts extensively, such as Command + Shift + Y to quickly access a workspace ready for merging. While he reviews the code generated by Claude, he often provides feedback. If Claude doesn't get things exactly right, Charlie leaves comments, similar to GitHub's style, asking clarifying questions like, "This looks a little bit weird to me. Why do we need this?" He then lets Claude run with the feedback and moves on to another workspace.

Experimentation is a core part of Charlie's process. He constantly kicks off new workspaces to explore different ideas, acknowledging that most don't make it into the final product. This iterative approach allows for rapid prototyping and testing of concepts, with successful experiments potentially being promoted through internal and experimental settings.

Conductor On the Go

Conductor is also extending its capabilities beyond the desktop. Charlie demonstrates the "on the go" feature by speaking into his phone: "Let's add a new feature where I can change the theme to hacker mode." After clicking "Conduct," his computer begins working on the request, enabling him to manage tasks remotely.

The Diminishing Role of Manual Coding

When asked if he still writes code manually, Charlie admits it's rare. His manual coding is typically limited to minor edits, such as adjusting Tailwind classes or modifying environment files. Conductor even includes a "caveman mode" for situations where direct keyboard input is necessary, but this is explicitly named to signify its infrequent use. For most small edits, he prefers to highlight code and provide verbal instructions to the AI or simply speak commands like, "That button looks a little too wide, can you make it smaller?"

The application's interface is designed for seamless integration. A workspace ready for merging can be archived with a single click, and completed tasks are moved into a "done" folder. A new "status" feature in the left panel tracks tasks through their lifecycle: "in progress," "in review" (once a PR is created), and "done" (after merging).

A new dashboard page aims to provide a centralized view of all agent activities, allowing users to direct their agents effectively. The ideal user experience is to feel like the CEO of a small company, overseeing agents who provide digestible reports and await direction for corrections or approval.

Essential Tools and Customizations

Charlie's daily software stack includes Telegram for communication with Claude and Spokenly for text-to-speech, running a local Parakeet model. His powerful computer, with 128GB of RAM, facilitates running local models. He also mentions ordering a low-spec MacBook to challenge himself to use minimal resources.

Key customizations that matter include meticulously crafted "skills files" and cloud.md documents. These contain hundreds of lines of instructions, including specific engineering practices tailored for a startup environment, differentiating their approach from enterprise coding.

He consistently uses "fast mode" for token efficiency and the "context 7 MCP" for documentation retrieval. A critical, non-default setting is "dangerously accept all permissions" when running Claude, which is central to Conductor's operational philosophy.

"Slop-Free" Zones and AI Boundaries

A core principle at Conductor is maintaining "slop-free zones" – areas of the codebase or documentation that are known to be human-written. While AI can contribute, every line must be human-reviewed. This prevents AI from entering a negative feedback loop, where it generates bad code based on previous AI-generated errors. Some codebases even include explicit "do not touch if you are an AI" directives.

Conductor's Tech Stack

Conductor is a Tauri app, utilizing the native Safari web renderer. The backend is written in Rust, but the desktop application is predominantly TypeScript (90-95%). The web app is a small Phoenix app built with Elixir, currently limited to login functionality, though Charlie expresses a strong preference for Elixir.

Guiding the AI: Don't Let It Be Your Architect

Charlie emphasizes that users should not let AI dictate architectural decisions. Concepts like Conductor's "workspace" feature, while an abstraction, were human-conceived. Similarly, UI/UX decisions, such as the layout of chats and sidebars, were carefully considered to create a crafted and intuitive experience. Allowing AI to make these design choices can lead to a less polished product.

A potential area for improvement is building the core of the app around human-written APIs and contracts, with distinct areas for AI experimentation. This separation ensures that AI exploration doesn't compromise core infrastructure. Conductor aims to push user comfort zones, a strategy that initially met with skepticism regarding managing multiple AI agents. The enforced workflow, where workspaces must create PRs for merging, was a deliberate choice to guide user interaction.

Adapting to Evolving Models

The challenge and excitement lie in adapting to the rapid advancements in AI models. As agents become more intelligent and capable of running for longer periods, the underlying infrastructure needs to evolve beyond the constraints of a user's local machine.

Building Conviction in Opinionated Design

Conductor's opinionated design approach is built on conviction, primarily through daily use by the development team. If something doesn't feel right, it's quickly addressed. This gut-feel approach, rather than relying heavily on analytics or A/B testing, ensures the product feels intuitive and cohesive.

Codex vs. Claude: Choosing the Right Tool

While Conductor supports both Codex and Claude, Charlie finds himself using Codex more frequently for its workhorse capabilities in tackling specific problems and performing extensive tool calls. Claude, on the other hand, is preferred for more creative, back-and-forth interactions, acting as a more collaborative partner, especially when building new features. Codex is the go-to for getting tasks done efficiently.

Beyond the Terminal: The GUI Advantage

The move from terminal interfaces to GUIs in the 1980s highlights humans' spatial and visual nature. Charlie argues that while AI might thrive in a command-line environment, humans benefit from visual cues. The ability to see chats on the left, the main interaction in the center, and a review panel on the right provides a more intuitive and less restrictive experience than a pure terminal interface. GUIs also unlock functionalities not possible in a terminal.

Token Maxing and Code Minimalism

Charlie shares a high token spend of $22,000 in July 2025 during Conductor's early development, using previous-generation models. He advocates for "token maxing" and using "fast mode" for high-effort interactions. However, Conductor aims to keep lines of code minimal to prevent codebase sprawl. The approach to code volume differs significantly between starting a new app and working on an established codebase like Conductor.

Workflow Evolution: Six Months of Change

Compared to six months prior, Charlie now relies less on opening an IDE for manual changes on complex PRs. He also uses the GitHub web app significantly less, as code review and commenting can be done directly within Conductor. The addition of a "checks tab" allows for integrating GitHub checks directly into Conductor.

Surprising Uses of Conductor

The most surprising use of Conductor was a user building a mobile version by spoofing inter-process communication calls to the desktop app. Gary, a power user, has also significantly pushed the boundaries of Conductor's capabilities, particularly with its "skills" feature. Conductor even has a "Gary mode" that keeps tool calls expanded for better visibility.

The Future of Collaboration and Code

Charlie believes the future lies in enhanced collaboration between humans and AI, including multiplayer chats where multiple users work with AI on the same task. The metaphor of conducting an orchestra resonates: guiding AI agents at a high level, intervening for specific adjustments, and allowing them to perform in unison.

Code, he suggests, is becoming akin to "sawdust" – a byproduct of the process rather than the primary focus. The emphasis is shifting from crafting code to describing desired outcomes. This means prompts become paramount, and with new model generations, rerunning prompts can yield updated code.

The concept of "malleable software," similar to modding video games, is key. Users should be able to customize Conductor with their own workflows, while the core structure remains polished and well-thought-out. This allows users to make the software feel truly their own.

item.title: Latent Space - Scaling Past Informal AI - Carina Hong, Axiom Math
item.description: Axiom Math, a seven-month-old company, has secured a significant $200 million Series A funding round, valuing the company at $1.6 billion. This substantial investment underscores a burgeoning belief i...
item.content:encoded:
Axiom Math: Scaling Brilliance with Verified AI

Axiom Math, a seven-month-old company, has secured a significant $200 million Series A funding round, valuing the company at $1.6 billion. This substantial investment underscores a burgeoning belief in the commercial viability of formal mathematics and verified AI, a domain traditionally viewed as niche. Axiom's mission extends beyond simply proving mathematical theorems; they aim to leverage formal methods to scale and compound superintelligence, fundamentally changing how we approach AI development and deployment.

The Math Startup Thesis: Beyond Pure Research

The substantial funding round prompts the question: how can a "math startup" justify such a valuation? Axiom's thesis, akin to Anthropic's early focus on coding, posits that structured and formal data, like that found in mathematics, offers a more horizontal and transferable advantage than initially perceived. While their core DNA is rooted in mathematics, with a mission to create AI that can act as a superhuman mathematician, their work has profound implications for verified reasoning and AI safety.

This approach contrasts with the common perception of formal verification as a tedious, compliance-driven process focused on eliminating errors and hallucinations. For Axiom, verified AI is about "scaling brilliance" and "compounding super intelligence." They draw parallels to mathematicians like Ramanujan, whose intuitive brilliance was amplified and transformed into theorems through the rigorous process of proof. Similarly, the structured, step-by-step logical deduction inherent in mathematical proofs, when translated into code, can serve as a powerful foundation for AI.

Verified AI: Scaling Brilliance, Not Fixing Lousiness

Formal verification, a field predating deep learning, has historically been applied in safety-critical domains like aerospace and critical infrastructure. However, Axiom views it not as a burden, but as a performance enhancer. They believe that verified generation leads to increased sample efficiency, allowing a startup with fewer resources than frontier labs to achieve or even surpass superhuman performance on complex tasks.

This was demonstrated by Axiom's performance on the Putnam exam, a prestigious undergraduate mathematics competition. While the best human score was 110 out of 120, Axiom achieved a perfect score of 120, outperforming even leading LLMs like DeepSeek, which scored 103. This achievement signifies a paradigm shift, suggesting that formal mathematical systems, despite potentially less training data, can rival or exceed the performance of informal LLMs.

Axiom's System: Lean Data, RL, and the Putnam Perfect Score

Axiom's success is built upon their heavy reliance on "Lean data" – proofs written in the Lean formal language. Lean is a computer program that acts as a formal language for mathematical proofs, similar to its cousins Coq and Isabelle. When a proof is written and compiled in Lean, and assuming no "weird things" like unchecked tactics are used, it is guaranteed to be correct. This is rooted in the Curry-Howard correspondence, which links proofs to programs.

Lean is a Turing-complete, functional programming language that can be used for both coding and mathematics. Axiom leverages Lean's capabilities by fine-tuning foundation models using Reinforcement Learning (RL) for formal mathematics. Their approach involves recursively decomposing proof goals into sub-goals and learning to backtrack, aiming to innovate beyond standard RL pipelines.

Mathematical Discovery: Before the Conjecture

Axiom is also investing heavily in "mathematical discovery" tools, which they plan to open-source. These tools are designed to assist mathematicians and theoretical physicists in formulating conjectures and constructions, a crucial pre-proving step. These tools can suggest interesting examples, sequences, or graph constructions, aiding in the initial stages of problem-solving. This initiative is spearheaded by team members with a history of solving complex mathematical problems, including counter-example finding for conjectures and solving long-standing problems like the global Leono function.

Rice's Theorem, Incompleteness, and Practical Limits

While theoretical limitations like Rice's Theorem suggest that not all programs can be formally verified, Axiom focuses on verifying the "majority of useful programs." Their vision is to integrate verification into the coding process, allowing AI to generate formally verified code components. This could range from front-end websites to more complex distributed systems. The goal is to provide a system where AI can either generate a formally verified program or state that a task is currently too difficult.

The process involves generating code and its corresponding proof. Axiom's performance on the Code Marina benchmark, a code verification benchmark, demonstrates their capability. While GPT-4 achieved a low pass rate, Axiom's system achieved a 99% success rate on code-with-proof generation, solving 187 out of 189 problems. This success is attributed to using Lean for proofs and a strongly typed language like Rust for code, creating a more cohesive objective function for RL.

Code With Proof — The Verina Benchmark

The Verina benchmark, developed by researchers from Berkeley and Meta, aims to evaluate AI's ability to generate code with accompanying proofs. Axiom's impressive performance highlights the potential of their approach. The challenge lies in ensuring that the generated code truly satisfies the verifiability conditions of the problem statement. While human mathematicians rely on peer review and consensus, Axiom's system leverages the formal guarantees of Lean to ensure correctness.

However, the problem of underspecification remains. Humans are notoriously bad at specifying every desired outcome, leading to potential gaps in formal specifications. Axiom acknowledges this limitation, stating, "Currently, the vision as of currently is anything that can be specified can be proven." They are exploring ways to address this through techniques like mutation-based LM unit test generation, which can help propose specifications.

Proof Trees, Context Windows, and Scaling Limits

The sheer size of formal proofs can be a challenge. Axiom notes that for every line of code, there can be up to 20 lines of proof. While they haven't established a definitive scaling law for this relationship, they believe their "Axium Improver," an ensemble system of post-trained models, can handle increasingly complex proof trees. They are not currently running into theoretical constraints, focusing instead on execution and scaling.

The problem of context windows in LLMs is also a consideration. Axiom's approach involves auto-formalization, where informal proofs are converted into formal ones, and then back into informal summaries. This process helps manage large proof structures and maintain consistency.

Collaboration, Polymath, and Human Attention as the Bottleneck

Axiom believes that verified AI will foster greater collaboration, moving beyond human-human collaboration to human-AI and even AI-AI collaboration. They see verification not as a barrier to entry for closed industries, but as a catalyst for openness. The desire to understand, driven by human curiosity, will ensure that even with AI-generated proofs, humans will continue to engage with and explore mathematical concepts. Human attention, rather than computational power, is seen as the ultimate bottleneck.

Founding Story — Obsession, Law School, and Julie Zhuo

Karina Hong's journey to founding Axiom Math is a testament to her deep-seated passion for mathematics and AI. After pursuing neuroscience at Oxford and AI research at UCL Gatsby, she enrolled in Stanford's JD-PhD program. During her year in law school, she found herself increasingly drawn to technology and the progress of AI, particularly in reasoning. This obsession, coupled with conversations with her CTO, Shoubo, led her to realize the immense potential of AI in mathematics.

A pivotal moment came when she met Julie Zhuo, a former Facebook PM, who advised her to "follow your energy." This led Hong to pivot from academia to entrepreneurship, founding Axiom Math with the belief that AI-driven mathematical reasoning would be a significant part of the future.

The Bigger Vision — AGI, Science, and Transfer Learning

Axiom's vision extends beyond mathematics to encompass AGI, AI for science, and even law. They believe that the structured reasoning developed through formal mathematics can unlock capabilities in diverse fields. The core of their strategy lies in the belief that advancements in math and formal verification will drive progress across scientific disciplines and beyond.

The company's success is attributed to its team of expert mathematicians who are also users of the system they are developing, creating a rapid iteration loop. This interdisciplinary approach, combining mathematical rigor with applied ML and codegen expertise, is seen as a key differentiator.

Key Takeaways

  • Verified AI for Scaling Brilliance: Axiom Math redefines formal verification not as a means to fix errors, but as a tool to scale and compound intelligence.
  • Math as a Horizontal Advantage: The structured nature of mathematics provides a powerful foundation for AI, with transferable applications beyond pure math.
  • Lean and Formal Proofs: Axiom leverages the Lean formal language to generate and verify mathematical proofs, ensuring correctness and enabling performance gains.
  • Mathematical Discovery: Axiom is developing tools to assist in the pre-conjecture stages of mathematical research, aiming to democratize discovery.
  • Code-with-Proof Generation: The company is pioneering the generation of code accompanied by formal proofs, a critical step towards trustworthy AI.
  • Human Attention as the Bottleneck: Despite AI's rapid progress, human curiosity and taste will remain crucial in guiding research and development.
  • Startup Advantage: Axiom believes that a focused startup environment allows for sustained dedication to long-term, ambitious goals, unlike larger organizations that may face shifting priorities.

item.title: The Pragmatic Engineer - Kubernetes and retiring at the top with Kelsey Hightower
item.description: Kelsey Hightower, a luminary in the Kubernetes community, has carved an unconventional path to the pinnacle of the tech industry. From dropping out of college to install DSL lines to becoming a distin...
item.content:encoded:
The Unconventional Path to Kubernetes Mastery and Early Retirement with Kelsey Hightower

Kelsey Hightower, a luminary in the Kubernetes community, has carved an unconventional path to the pinnacle of the tech industry. From dropping out of college to install DSL lines to becoming a distinguished engineer at Google and eventually retiring at 43, his journey is a testament to self-taught skills, strategic career moves, and a deep understanding of technology's human element. This article delves into his early life, his non-traditional entry into tech, the evolution of infrastructure automation, the rise of Kubernetes, and his pragmatic approach to AI and career longevity.

From McDonald's to the Digital Frontier

Hightower's first foray into the professional world was at McDonald's, where he quickly ascended to assistant manager by age 15, learning the intricacies of responsibility and efficiency. His introduction to technology came during a move to Atlanta, where a required computer programming class sparked an unexpected interest. He excelled in AutoCAD, even competing at the state level, and discovered the programmable nature of TI graphing calculators, a humble beginning for a future tech leader.

A Non-Traditional Path into Tech

Despite a strong academic record that qualified him for free college tuition through Georgia's HOPE program, Hightower found the pace of university too slow. Graduating in 1999, amidst the dot-com boom's euphoria, he was drawn to the industry's rapid evolution and the narratives of college dropouts achieving success. Lacking connections to the burgeoning tech world, he pursued a more accessible route: A+ certification. This self-directed learning, fueled by a $35 book and a desire for immediate feedback, provided the foundational knowledge to enter the tech industry.

The Genesis of a Tech Career

His first official tech role involved installing DSL internet for Bell South. This hands-on experience, from cabling to troubleshooting Windows 98 crashes, provided invaluable practical knowledge. When faced with the challenge of connecting multiple computers to a single internet connection for businesses, Hightower took the initiative to learn, purchasing a Linksys router and figuring out the solution. This entrepreneurial spirit led him to start his own computer repair and sales business, "Digital Gateways," at just 19, learning the fundamentals of business licensing and operations.

Entrepreneurship and the Road to Google

Hightower's entrepreneurial ventures expanded to managing a comedian, which took him on the road and exposed him to the entertainment industry's IT needs. This period, while demanding, allowed him to save money and hone his business acumen. The realization that the growth trajectory of a service-based business differed from software companies prompted a shift. He sought a more stable career path and, recognizing Google's nearby data centers and his diverse skill set, applied for a data center technician role. His knowledge of FreeBSD, rather than Linux, proved to be a fortunate connection during the interview, securing him a position.

Mastering Automation and the Rise of Configuration Management

At Google, Hightower was immersed in a highly systematic and data-driven environment. He excelled as a data center technician, driven by a competitive spirit and a desire to master every aspect of the operation. This led him to explore various roles within the company, each jump resulting in a significant pay increase and expanded skill set.

A pivotal moment came with his introduction to Puppet Labs. He witnessed firsthand the inefficiencies of manual IT operations and embraced configuration management tools like Puppet as a way to automate and standardize deployments. This period marked his transition from a hands-on technician to a software engineer, contributing to open-source projects and laying the groundwork for his future involvement in DevOps.

The Container Revolution and the Birth of Kubernetes

Hightower's journey continued through web hosting companies, where he learned the importance of automation and end-to-end system management. He experienced a significant inflection point at a Rackspace spinoff, where he learned to automate processes like firmware updates and OS installations, realizing the power of end-to-end automation. He also learned the critical difference between activity and impact, understanding that reducing the ticket queue to zero had a far greater impact than simply answering calls.

The advent of containers, particularly Docker, marked a paradigm shift. While initially dismissed by some at Puppet Labs as a fad, Hightower recognized its potential. He saw how Docker simplified application packaging and deployment, eliminating the complexities of managing dependencies and environments. This led him to explore tools like CoreOS, an operating system designed specifically for containers, and eventually, Kubernetes.

The Kubernetes Phenomenon

Hightower's deep involvement with CoreOS positioned him perfectly to embrace Kubernetes upon its release. He contributed significantly to making Kubernetes accessible on CoreOS, even working overnight to get it running and publishing guides that demystified the new orchestration system. His keynote talks, initially about CoreOS, quickly pivoted to Kubernetes, showcasing its power and potential.

He attributes Kubernetes' breakthrough success to several key factors:

  • Docker's Ubiquity: Docker had already established a de facto standard for containerization, providing a ready-made ecosystem for Kubernetes to leverage.
  • Strategic Simplicity: Kubernetes wisely adopted existing, proven technologies like etcd for distributed coordination and Docker as its container runtime, rather than reinventing the wheel.
  • Lessons from Borg: The Kubernetes team, drawing from Google's extensive experience with its internal cluster management system, Borg, brought invaluable insights into large-scale distributed systems.
  • Filling the Gaps: Kubernetes addressed critical shortcomings in Docker Swarm, such as robust storage and networking management, and provided a more structured approach to application architecture through its declarative model and process tree management.
  • Infrastructure as Data: Kubernetes shifted the paradigm from imperative "if this, then that" infrastructure management to a declarative "desired state" model, where users define the desired outcome, and Kubernetes works to achieve it.
  • Extensibility: The ability to extend Kubernetes with custom resource definitions (CRDs) opened the door for a vast ecosystem of tools and integrations, allowing it to manage diverse infrastructure components beyond just containers.

A Pragmatic Approach to AI and the Future of Software Engineering

Hightower's perspective on AI is grounded in his people-first philosophy. He views AI not as a replacement for human ingenuity but as a powerful tool that, when used strategically, can augment human capabilities. He emphasizes the importance of understanding the underlying problems and using AI to solve them effectively, rather than blindly adopting it.

His advice to experienced software engineers facing the rise of AI is to:

  • Reflect on Past Automation: Recognize that software engineering has always been about automation and that the current wave of AI is a continuation of this trend.
  • Understand Your Core Job: Identify what your role truly entails beyond just writing code. If your value is solely in code generation, that aspect may become commoditized. However, skills in architecture, design, customer interaction, and problem-solving remain crucial.
  • Embrace AI as a Tool: Learn to leverage AI tools to enhance productivity and focus on higher-level decision-making, design, and innovation.
  • Go Deeper: For those seeking to remain at the forefront, a deep understanding of fundamental principles, hardware, and system design will be invaluable for creating novel solutions and pushing the boundaries of what's possible.
  • Focus on Human Problems: Remember that the ultimate goal of software engineering is to solve human problems. AI can help achieve this more efficiently, but the human element of understanding needs and context remains paramount.

The Art of Advising, Investing, and Intentional Living

Having retired from full-time employment, Hightower now dedicates his time to advising startups, investing, and public speaking. He advocates for a pragmatic approach to advisory roles, emphasizing the importance of tangible impact and fair compensation, whether through equity or retainers. His experience with companies like Pixie Labs and Verscell highlights the value of strategic guidance and a deep understanding of technology trends.

His philosophy extends to his personal life, where he practices minimalism and intentionality. He views money as "freedom tokens" that enable him to live below his means and pursue his passions. This disciplined approach to finances, coupled with a focus on relationships and personal growth, has allowed him to achieve early retirement and a fulfilling life beyond the confines of traditional employment.

Kelsey Hightower's journey is a powerful reminder that success in technology is not solely defined by traditional career paths or degrees. It is forged through curiosity, continuous learning, a willingness to embrace change, and a deep commitment to solving real-world problems with a human-centric approach.

item.title: Y Combinator - How to Build an AI-Native Services Company
item.description: The next decade's most impactful companies may not be traditional software giants, but rather AI-native services firms. These businesses, built from the ground up with artificial intelligence handling...
item.content:encoded:
🎥 Watch on YouTube: How to Build an AI-Native Services Company

The AI-Native Services Company: A Playbook for Founders

The next decade's most impactful companies may not be traditional software giants, but rather AI-native services firms. These businesses, built from the ground up with artificial intelligence handling the bulk of the work, are poised to disrupt trillion-dollar markets like tax, audit, insurance, law, and parts of healthcare. This new paradigm, unlocked by recent advancements in AI models, allows companies to provide direct outcomes to customers rather than simply building co-pilot tools for internal use. This article outlines a playbook for founders looking to establish these novel AI services businesses, covering market selection, team formation, product development, customer service, financial management, and strategic acquisition.

Picking the Right Market

While the general advice for startup founders—choosing a market you're passionate about for the long haul—still applies, AI services markets have four unique characteristics that are crucial for success.

First, low trust is a key indicator. This means the work is already frequently outsourced, and clients prioritize the final outcome over the internal process. You're essentially displacing an existing vendor rather than forcing customers to adopt entirely new behaviors. This allows you to tap into existing budgets and workflows.

Second, low judgment at the task level is essential for scalability. If every step of the work requires significant human judgment, scaling becomes difficult. The ideal scenario involves automating most tasks, with human judgment concentrated in a few critical areas where they remain in the loop.

Third, a high intelligence threshold is necessary. The overall work must be complex enough that a combination of AI and human expertise is required to deliver an outcome that satisfies the customer. This complexity prevents commoditization by AI alone.

Fourth, regulation can be an advantage. Regulated industries often have higher expectations and legal accountability, which can create a stronger moat for founders. For example, Panacea, an AI native company, provides FDA regulatory services for biotechs and medtechs by pairing experienced FDA consultants with an AI platform to expedite and improve the quality of regulatory approvals.

Established markets like tax, audit, insurance, mortgages, and segments of healthcare and logistics are known to be good fits. However, numerous untapped markets exist beyond these obvious choices.

The Sam Altman Test and Founding Teams

When evaluating potential markets, consider the Sam Altman Test: as AI models improve, does your service become stronger, or does the model itself commoditize your offering? You want to be in the first camp. Be cautious of markets heavily reliant on equipment or on-site labor, as the software margin math doesn't easily apply when physical assets are involved.

Another critical self-assessment is to honestly determine if you're using humans because the work genuinely requires judgment, or if you're compensating for product gaps. While great technology businesses can be built with humans in the loop, transparency about this distinction is vital.

Beyond the market, the right founding team is paramount. As with any startup, building with trusted colleagues is ideal. For AI services, however, three specific attributes are critical for success:

  1. Domain Fluency: Direct experience in the target industry is best, but deep learned knowledge is also acceptable. Selling to skeptical buyers, especially in regulated sectors, requires inherent credibility.
  2. Model Fluency: A thorough understanding of current frontier model capabilities and the ability to design a product that evolves with AI advancements is non-negotiable. Underestimating the importance of cutting-edge technology here is a common pitfall.
  3. Operational Rigor: Founders must embrace concepts like variance, throughput, and cycle times. Running an AI services business is fundamentally an operational challenge. Skills in managing these aspects are crucial, even if they aren't the most glamorous.

The General Legal team, an AI-native law firm backed by YC, exemplifies this. Its founders combine extensive law firm experience with technical leadership, and they deeply consider throughput and staffing. They've integrated shift work to reduce cycle times and attract top talent, demonstrating a commitment to operational excellence.

Building the Product: The Human as the Interface

In AI services, the product's architecture is inverted compared to traditional software. The human operator serves as the primary interface with the customer, and the product's role is to scale that human's work nonlinearly. This fundamental difference shapes the product development process.

An operations mindset is essential. Identify bottlenecks and build solutions for them, treating throughput and cycle time as core product metrics, akin to daily active users in software.

Variance is the existential threat. Non-uniform outputs erode customer trust faster than slower speeds or higher costs. Inconsistency leads to churn.

Humans in the loop must be able to scale nonlinearly. If revenue growth directly correlates with the number of humans added, significant problems will arise. Furthermore, the human operators are your users, and they need to find the software enjoyable and efficient. While starting with tasks that don't scale is acceptable, long-term scalability through automation is the ultimate goal.

Sales, Customer Success, and Pricing

The biggest challenge founders face is the early demand trap. While it's easy to secure numerous pilot customers initially, this can quickly overwhelm capacity and hinder product development. The advice is to cap the number of early pilot customers to a manageable handful.

Once past this trap, pre- and post-sales approaches differ significantly. You must sell outcomes, not seats or tokens. The pilot itself is the product. For initial customers, avoid premature standardization; use these pilots to learn, identify areas where AI provides unique leverage, and build the product accordingly and rapidly.

Pricing is more complex than in traditional software, as you're competing directly with the cost of labor, whether internal or outsourced.

  • Per-unit pricing (e.g., per return, per claim, per loan) is often the cleanest and easiest to explain.
  • Outcome-based pricing aligns incentives effectively but can be harder to forecast. Panacea, for instance, prices on completed consultant studies rather than hourly rates, which is the industry norm.

Two pricing strategies to avoid are:

  • Cost-plus pricing, which permanently caps your upside.
  • Straight-line undercutting, which devalues your service and can imply low quality.

Always price on value.

The P&L Walkthrough: The Heart of the Business

The profit and loss (P&L) statement is where AI services companies truly live or die. Understanding its components is crucial:

  • Revenue: While seemingly straightforward, the challenge lies in consistent delivery. Early revenue may be spiky, but a robust product and process will lead to smoother, predictable growth.
  • Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): This is a critical area to obsess over from day one. It comprises model costs, hosting costs, and the cost of humans in the loop. Each component needs clear metrics, trend lines, and ownership. Be wary of zero or negative margin pilots; they are useful for learning but dangerous if relied upon. The core bet is that as the product matures, COGS decrease, leading to higher gross margins—this is known as AI operating leverage.
  • Operating Expenses (Opex): This includes research and development (product building) and sales, general, and administrative costs (finance, legal, executive salaries).
  • Operating Income: This is revenue minus COGS and Opex. Founders will be judged on operating income relatively quickly.
  • Net Income: Operating income less taxes and interest, less critical in the medium term.

The P&L opportunity for these companies is substantial. Traditional services firms typically top out around 30% margins. Pure software companies offer higher margins but often have smaller total addressable markets (TAMs). AI services companies aim to achieve software-like margins (50%+) on larger markets by leveraging AI operating leverage. While immediate high margins aren't expected, a believable trajectory towards them is essential.

Don't Buy Your Way In
